items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: In a standard hydrogen electrode used as a reference, what is the assigned electrode potential?
items.correct: Exactly 0 V by convention — all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.
items.misconception: The standard hydrogen electrode actually has zero potential because hydrogen has no charge.
items.random_wrong: The standard hydrogen electrode has a potential of -1 V to balance copper at +1 V.
items.why: Students don't realise 0 V is a chosen reference, not a measured value; they think hydrogen is genuinely neutral electrochemically.

items.q: Which of the following is a property of most metals?
items.why: Correct answer: Shiny. “Brittle” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Sulfur. “Iron” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature (approx 20°C)? Pick its atomic number: 80 = Mercury, 26 = Iron, 29 = Copper, 79 = Gold.
items.why: The real figure is 80. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
